does: RiskNet constructs a large-scale dataset of real-world AI risk incidents by aggregating and structuring reports from multilingual news sources. The paper develops a systematic pipeline that identifies AI-related news, screens reports at the event level, aligns multiple reports to common incidents, and applies multi-dimensional risk classification to organize dispersed news into incident-centered records. The dataset consolidates diverse harms and failures of AI systems deployed in consequential domains, providing benchmark datasets for downstream computational tasks including event classification, incident alignment, and risk labeling at the incident level.
connects: This work responds to a gap in empirical resources for AI governance and safety monitoring. While prior frameworks articulate responsible AI principles, existing incident collections remain manual, small-scale, and insufficient for data-driven analysis. RiskNet builds on work in harm documentation and AI audit methodologies by applying automated and semi-automated techniques borrowed from information extraction and event detection in NLP. It extends previous incident tracking efforts by introducing multi-dimensional annotation schemes and scale, connecting to broader efforts in computational social science that extract structured knowledge from unstructured text at scale.
enables: RiskNet enables continuous, scalable monitoring of AI-related risks across global news sources, supporting data-driven governance and policy development. The benchmark datasets facilitate machine learning research on incident detection and classification, potentially automating the identification of emerging AI harms in real time. The structured incident records enable longitudinal analysis of risk patterns, geographic distributions, and harm severity trends. By connecting news reports to unified incidents, the resource supports causal analysis of systemic failures and could enable early warning systems for concerning AI deployment patterns, supporting regulators, researchers, and safety practitioners in evidence-based decision-making.
